metadata.summary: Automate security patch management on Ubuntu 24.04 with unattended-upgrades: enable daily security updates, schedule auto-reboot at 4 AM, blacklist Docker/databases, clean up old kernels.

content.title: Unattended Upgrades
content.summary: Automate security patch management on Ubuntu 24.04 with `unattended-upgrades`: enable daily security-origin updates, schedule auto-reboot at off-peak hours (4 AM), blacklist packages that require manual intervention (Docker, databases), and clean up old kernels automatically. Services must have `Restart=always` and Docker containers `restart: unless-stopped` to recover after automatic reboots.

why.p: Unpatched servers are the leading cause of security breaches, and manual patching is easily forgotten by a solo developer. The Ubuntu security pocket receives patches for known CVEs within hours — automatic installation closes the window between disclosure and exploitation without requiring manual SSH sessions.

list.item: Any production Ubuntu VPS that should stay patched without manual intervention
Servers running kernel-level services where CVEs appear frequently (OpenSSL, libc, systemd)
Configuring auto-reboot after kernel updates to apply them
Auditing whether existing auto-update config is correct
During initial server setup before verifying all services have `Restart=always` — auto-reboot will leave services down
For database major version upgrades — blacklist the package and upgrade manually after testing
When using a managed platform that handles patching (Heroku, Railway) — don't install unattended-upgrades there
For Docker CE — version changes can break container runtimes; always blacklist and upgrade manually

section.name: update-origins
section.p: Only enable security pockets. This applies CVE patches without installing feature updates that could break production services.

rule.statement: Unattended-Upgrade::Allowed-Origins MUST include only security pockets: `${distro_id}:${distro_codename}-security` and ESM security endpoints. Never include -updates or -backports.
rule.rationale: -updates contains non-security bug fixes and minor version bumps. These can change behavior unexpectedly in production without a maintenance window.

rule.statement: Blacklist Docker CE, database engines, and any package whose upgrade requires manual intervention.
rule.rationale: Docker CE version changes can break container networking and require daemon restart. Database major versions require migration scripts. Automatic upgrade of these during the night causes silent production outages.

rule.statement: Enable auto-reboot at an off-peak time (4 AM typical). Without auto-reboot, kernel security patches are installed but never applied.
rule.rationale: Kernel, libc, and systemd patches only take effect after reboot. A server can run with a patched kernel on disk but still be vulnerable via the loaded (old) kernel in memory for weeks.

rule.statement: All systemd services must have Restart=always and Docker containers must have restart: unless-stopped so they recover after the automatic reboot.
rule.rationale: Auto-reboot is only safe if services reliably restart. A service without Restart=always stays down after the reboot until someone SSHes in to start it manually.

example.title: Kernel Update Timeline
example.code:
03:00 apt-daily-upgrade runs 03:01 linux-image-6.8.0-107 installed 03:01 /var/run/reboot-required created 03:01 /var/run/reboot-required.pkgs: linux-image-6.8.0-107
04:00 Automatic-Reboot-Time triggers reboot 04:01 Server boots with new kernel 04:02 Services restart via Restart=always 04:03 All services active, new kernel running

section.name: cleanup-directives
section.p: Remove unused dependencies, unused kernel packages, and auto-fix interrupted dpkg operations.

section.name: schedule-20auto-upgrades
section.p: Two config files control unattended-upgrades: 50unattended-upgrades (what to update, blacklist, reboot policy, cleanup) and 20auto-upgrades (schedule). Two systemd timers drive execution: apt-daily (update package lists) and apt-daily-upgrade (install). Override timers for exact scheduling.

section.name: verification
section.p: Always verify configuration with --dry-run before relying on automatic execution.

example.code:
# Dry-run: show what would be upgraded sudo unattended-upgrade --dry-run --debug 2>&1 | head -40
# Expected output for a clean run: # Packages that will be upgraded: libssl3 openssl # NOT Unattended-Upgrades-kept-back: docker-ce (blacklisted) # Packages that will be removed: (none)

example.title: Verifying Docker Packages Are Held Back
example.code: sudo unattended-upgrade --dry-run --debug 2>&1 | grep -E "Kept|blacklist|docker" # Expected: # NOT Unattended-Upgrades-kept-back: docker-ce is blacklisted # NOT Unattended-Upgrades-kept-back: docker-ce-cli is blacklisted

example.title: Checking Reboot-Required Status
example.code:
# Quick check [ -f /var/run/reboot-required ] && echo "REBOOT NEEDED" || echo "OK"
# Which packages need it cat /var/run/reboot-required.pkgs
# Last upgrade timestamp sudo stat /var/log/unattended-upgrades/unattended-upgrades.log | grep Modify

template.purpose: Production config: security origins, Docker blacklist, auto-reboot at 04:00, cleanup
Schedule: daily update check, download, install, weekly autoclean
Fix apt-daily timer to exact time (2 AM)
Fix apt-daily-upgrade timer to exact time (3 AM)

script.purpose: Report reboot-required status, packages needing it, auto-reboot config
Dashboard: config state, pending updates, recent log, timer schedule, blacklist
